control_receiving.description: The first touch is the most important skill in football. Receiving the ball cleanly sets up every subsequent action.

techniques.name: Inside of Foot
techniques.description: Use the inside of the foot to bring a ground ball under control. All body weight on the supporting leg with receiving foot raised slightly off the ground.
steps: Stand in a relaxed position with eyes focused on the ball
Adjust position to remain in the flight of the ball
Place non-kicking foot slightly in front
Meet the ball and immediately withdraw foot on impact
Raise foot 4–5 inches off the ground, toes pulled up, foot parallel to ground

techniques.name: Cushioning
techniques.description: Absorb pace by meeting the ball with a cushioned touch. Withdraw the controlling surface as contact is made.
steps: Stand lightly as ball approaches
Position in ball's line of travel
Assess options and choose controlling body surface
Look directly at the ball
Position non-kicking foot ahead of ball
Use arms for balance
Bring striking foot back at moment of impact

techniques.name: Outside of Foot
techniques.description: Trapping foot brought in front or across the plant leg so outside of foot faces the ball. Toe pointed slightly down, ankle locked.

techniques.name: Thigh
techniques.description: Raise thigh to 90-degree angle. As ball contacts thigh, drop knee toward ground to create cushioning effect.

techniques.name: Chest
techniques.description: Stand inline with flight of ball. Use arms for balance, bend backward to get chest underneath ball. Bend knees slightly to absorb momentum.

ball_control_principles: First touch protects ball from challenging players
Play ball into available space to allow for next touch
Allow game movement to start or continue by gaining or keeping momentum

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball bounces away after receiving
faults_and_fixes.fix: Let the ball come and just as it is received, pull foot back slightly
faults_and_fixes.drill: Roll or toss ball slowly at first, increase speed until player is comfortable

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball goes under foot
faults_and_fixes.fix: Watch ball all the way to foot. Let the ball hit foot

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball path misread, player misses pass
faults_and_fixes.fix: Repeated practice using passes from different distances. Keep eyes on ball at all times, get body in line

faults_and_fixes.fault: Player indecisive choosing controlling surface
faults_and_fixes.fix: Player shouts type of body surface they will use before server provides pass

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball bounces off player, control lost
faults_and_fixes.fix: Exaggerate the relaxation movement. Start with gentle service, build up difficulty. Try smaller or lighter balls

coaching_tips: Help lower ability players move into position and get behind the flight of the ball
Encourage players to slow down, relax and concentrate — as important as coaching correct technique
Have players receive the ball with different parts of their body — thigh, knee and chest
Emphasize the importance of the first touch
Give positive reinforcement to players that control the ball
Play a make-shift game allowing players only two touches

dribbling.description: Dribbling is running with the ball at your feet — speeding through open space, maneuvering out of tight situations and feinting to beat opponents.

techniques.name: Top of Foot (Laces)
techniques.description: Dribble forward using the top of the foot along the outside of the laces. Point toe down toward ground. Keep ball within one metre.

techniques.name: Inside of Foot
techniques.description: Use inside of foot to quickly change direction and beat opponent. Short, even strides while keeping ball close.

techniques.name: Outside of Foot
techniques.description: Shield the ball from opponents and evade tackles. Keeps player in position to pass to teammate.

techniques.name: Sole of Foot
techniques.description: Change ball direction, then move ball to outside of foot.

faults_and_fixes.fault: Cannot control ball when running
faults_and_fixes.fix: Player walks and dribbles. Slowly increase to light jog, then to a run
faults_and_fixes.drill: Dribble around half of the field while running

faults_and_fixes.fault: Loses ball when moving right and/or left
faults_and_fixes.fix: Player slowly dribbles around cones. Increase speed as touch improves
faults_and_fixes.drill: Timed cone dribbling — award point for best time or best change in time

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball always runs away from player
faults_and_fixes.fix: Slow down. Have pace related to ability

coaching_tips: Work individually with low-energy players using gentle physical prompting (hand on middle of back)
For high-energy players who kick ball too far, start by walking and concentrate on control before speeding up
For players who have mastered dribbling, have them dribble with head up, viewing the field
Encourage use of individual tricks — dummies, stopovers, turns, dragging ball back
Encourage players to dribble to create space
Players need to be aware of teammates' positions before dribbling

passing.description: Football is a team game. Through passing, a team can counterattack, maintain possession and set up scoring opportunities. 70% of passing is made with the inside of the foot.

techniques.name: Inside of Foot (Push Pass)
techniques.description: For short passes. Strike firmly with foot following through in direction of intended receiver.
steps: Square up so player, ball and target are in a straight line
Place plant foot about 4 inches from side of ball, pointing toward target
Head over the ball, eyes looking down, both knees slightly bent
Place striking foot flat against back of ball, 5 inches above ground
Push ball toward target, following through (not a jab)
Ball contact with toes pulled up, foot parallel to ground, ankle locked
Make contact with arch below ankle bone, follow through for top spin

techniques.name: Instep (Long Pass)
techniques.description: Use instep to send longer passes with greater power.

organizing_tips: Control with one foot, pass with same foot
Control with one foot, pass with other foot
Control with outside of one foot, pass with inside of same foot
Control with inside of one foot, pass with outside of same foot

faults_and_fixes.fault: Frustrated player not receiving passes
faults_and_fixes.fix: Players put themselves in good position to receive pass and call for ball
faults_and_fixes.drill: Encourage players to move around the field when they don't have the ball

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball goes in the air
faults_and_fixes.fix: Show player correct ball contact. Make contact with ball higher

faults_and_fixes.fault: Player stumbles after kick
faults_and_fixes.fix: Show player correct ball contact. Make contact with ball lower

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball goes to the side of teammate
faults_and_fixes.fix: Striking foot not square to balancing foot. Correct player's stance

coaching_tips: Always begin teaching passing with side of foot first
Have receiver stand close, then move further as passing improves
The major problem with most players is not looking for teammates before passing
Coach can freeze play in practice games to allow player to look for teammate
Practice at home using a wall or fence to perfect technique
When on offense, pass from outside of field to inside of field
Always pass the ball in front of your teammate

shooting.description: The aim of the game is to score goals. Players need good technique and a positive attitude toward taking shots whenever they have a good opportunity.

techniques.name: Instep Shot
techniques.description: For power and accuracy. Approach from side. Head down, ankle firmly locked, strike with instep.
steps: Approach ball slightly from the side, not straight on
Place non-kicking foot at side of ball
Swing kicking foot backward with bent knee
Keep eye on ball and head still
Swing kicking foot forward with knee pointing down
Kicking foot follows ball as knee straightens
Follow through guides ball to corner of net

techniques.name: Low Shot (Laces)
techniques.description: Hit ball with laces of shoe for effective low shots.

coaching_principles: Most goals scored from side of goal — teach players to shoot at an angle
It is harder for goalkeeper to save a low shot
Always aim for far post when shooting at an angle
When shooting, accuracy is more important than power
Teach shooting in correct size goals

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball has back spin when shot or goes high/wide
faults_and_fixes.fix: Encourage player to strike the middle of the ball. Listen for the 'boom' when ball is struck properly

faults_and_fixes.fault: Player cannot get power or accuracy when shooting
faults_and_fixes.fix: Make sure player plants non-kicking foot next to ball. Make sure knee on kicking leg is over the ball. Kick through the ball
faults_and_fixes.drill: Have player stand with non-kicking foot next to ball and phantom kick

coaching_tips: As a general rule, teach players to control the ball before trying to shoot
Encourage players to shoot toward corners of goal, not middle
Do not be afraid to shoot even if not close to goal — good things can happen
Play one-on-one games to perfect forward play or defense

tackling.description: Tackling uses your feet to take the ball away from an opponent. A committed, determined and cool-headed approach is essential.
key_words: See Your Foot Strike The Ball
Make Contact - Then Make It Yours!

types.name: Front Block Tackle
types.description: When defender meets attacker head-on with body square to ball. Forceful contact with inside of boot.
coaching_tips: Keep eye on ball
Upper body weight forward
Coordinate body movement
Apply maximum force, aiming to block the ball, not kick it

types.name: Side Block Tackle
types.description: When attacker has partially gone past defender or defender tries to close down angled pass. Requires good balance with outstretched leg.
coaching_tips: Keep eye on ball
Teach athletes not to sell themselves as the attacker
Touch shoulder to shoulder before tackling to reduce risk

types.name: Ground Block Tackle
types.description: When block or side tackle cannot be used. Defender throws body into ball. Stay on feet as long as possible.
coaching_tips: Keep eye on ball
Be patient
Use only as last resort
Get back on feet as quickly as possible

types.name: Sliding Tackle
types.description: Last resort to prevent attacker getting past. Defender slides on ground into ball, playing it with outside leg.
coaching_tips: Always made from the side of the dribbler
All contact on the ball
Very useful if player has been beaten
Can be dangerous if not done correctly — use with experienced players only

faults_and_fixes.fault: Defender gets beat because of giving too much space
faults_and_fixes.fix: Stay close to opposing players and run with them. Concentrate on the player being defended

faults_and_fixes.fault: Defender chases player and cannot catch opponent
faults_and_fixes.fix: Always stay between your goal and player. Teach positional sense

faults_and_fixes.fault: Opposition player runs past because of missed tackle
faults_and_fixes.fix: Time tackle so you are sure of winning ball. Do not commit too early

coaching_tips: Cue beginner players to 'slow down' and 'concentrate on ball' when approaching opponent
Organize players in pairs with a ball to practice 50/50 ball situations
Encourage players to run through tackles by keeping legs moving in close contact
Encourage players to jockey until the time is right to tackle
Do not play defenders too far back — no more than 5-metre cushion
Only tackle when you think you will win the ball

heading.description: Approximately 20% of goals come from headed shots. Players must be able to pass, shoot, clear and control the ball using their head.

types.name: Directional Header
types.description: Control the ball (pass, shoot or receive). Strike with forehead just below or at hairline where player can see the ball.

types.name: Clearing Header
types.description: Send ball as far as possible. Strike with forehead at hairline or top of head. Defender often leaps for more power.

types.name: Flick Header
types.description: Jump for ball to play or continue it in more or less the same direction. Useful for center forwards receiving long passes.

steps: Keep eyes open until impact, fixed on ball
Position body inline with ball
Rock onto back foot before ball arrives
Make contact with forehead (not top of head)
Attack through ball for power
Power comes from legs, back and neck — keep wide base, staggered stance
Run and jump off one leg to meet ball
Good timing of run and anticipation of where ball will be
Upper body arches backward after takeoff
Body propelled forward — snapping action
Neck extended then snapped forward in synchronization
Strike ball at TOP of jump — jumping too early loses all power
Drive non-jumping leg up toward ball when jumping

heading.safety_note: Medical studies have found that extensive heading can cause brain damage. Do not teach headers until U-10. Do not stress them until U-11. Use soft or underinflated ball when teaching.

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball hits player's head and goes in any direction
faults_and_fixes.fix: Eyes must focus on ball. Coach serves ball ensuring correct contact is made

faults_and_fixes.fault: No direction in header
faults_and_fixes.fix: Place feet in correct position. Coach demonstrates correct timing

faults_and_fixes.fault: Player refuses to head ball
faults_and_fixes.fix: Build up confidence using lighter ball. Motivate. Show correct technique

coaching_tips: Overcome fear by working one-on-one with player heading stationary ball from coach's hand
Deflate ball a little or use sponge/foam ball at first
Higher ability players can begin to jump to head rather than waiting for ball
Use verbal cue to help player time the jump just before ball arrives
Power in heading comes from trunk, legs and arms — not from snapping neck
Explain that trunk arches backward prior to contacting ball whether on ground or in air
For nervous players, teach them to deflect ball to another player using chest

goalkeeping.description: Goalkeeping protects your goal from opponent's shots. A sound, confident goalkeeper is the source of confidence for the entire team.
selection_criteria: Confidence — bold character willing to throw into penalty area
Communication — able to organize players and call clearly when leaving line
Handling — safe hands more important than height
Distribution — must be confident ball kicker who can kick long distance
ready_position: Stand lightly on balls of feet
Hands at waist height with palms opened

techniques.name: Handling High Balls
techniques.description: Form a 'W' with thumbs almost touching. Gets hands behind ball for safe stopping.

techniques.name: Handling Low Balls
techniques.description: Form an 'M' with little fingers almost touching. Get hands behind ball consistently.

techniques.name: Diving for Ground Ball
techniques.description: Lower center of gravity before diving. Dive on side of body (not flat on stomach). Get both hands to ball then immediately bring into body.
steps: Crouch and bend knees to lower center of gravity
Dive on side of body — increases barrier and is safer
Get both hands to ball
Bring securely into body immediately

techniques.name: Distribution — Throwing
techniques.description: Look wide to wings, not middle. Use underhand (bowling), overhand (baseball) or two-handed throw-in style. Distribute quickly before opponents regroup.

techniques.name: Distribution — Punting
techniques.description: In 11-a-side, most effective distribution for Special Olympics goalkeepers. Always kick to wing, not middle.

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper uses only one hand to catch the ball
faults_and_fixes.fix: Always use two hands — form a 'W' for high balls (thumbs almost touching) and an 'M' for low balls (little fingers almost touching). Practice catching with both hands together

faults_and_fixes.fault: Ball drops or spills out of hands after catch
faults_and_fixes.fix: After catching, immediately hug ball into chest. Get both hands behind ball and pull into body before any movement

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper dives flat on stomach when diving for ground balls
faults_and_fixes.fix: Always dive on the SIDE of the body, not the stomach. Lower your centre of gravity first by bending knees, then go down on the side — it is safer and creates a bigger barrier

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper stands flat-footed and is slow to react
faults_and_fixes.fix: Always stay on the balls of your feet — never flat-footed. Keep knees slightly bent and hands at waist height ready to move in any direction

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper stands on or too near the goal line
faults_and_fixes.fix: Come one or two steps off the goal line. This gives better angle coverage and makes it harder for attackers to chip over you

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper throws ball straight up the middle
faults_and_fixes.fix: Always distribute WIDE to the wings. Look left or right first, never throw into crowded central areas where opponents can intercept

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper distributes too slowly — opponents regroup
faults_and_fixes.fix: Distribute quickly once you have the ball secure. Look up first, pick your target on the wing, and throw or kick immediately

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper does not communicate with defenders
faults_and_fixes.fix: Call loudly and early — shout 'KEEPER!' when coming for the ball, 'AWAY!' to tell defenders to clear, and call players by name when distributing

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper comes off line too early on one-on-one
faults_and_fixes.fix: Come out slowly at first to narrow the angle, then hold your position. Do not dive too early — wait for the attacker to commit before making your move

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper watches feet instead of the ball
faults_and_fixes.fix: Eyes must be FIXED on the ball at all times. Watch the ball from the moment it leaves the attacker's foot all the way into your hands

faults_and_fixes.fault: Poor positioning — goalkeeper not central between posts
faults_and_fixes.fix: Always check your position relative to both posts. Use your feet to feel the line and keep glancing back to adjust. Stay in the centre of an imaginary line between the ball and the goal centre

faults_and_fixes.fault: Goalkeeper loses concentration when ball is at the other end
faults_and_fixes.fix: Keep scanning the field at all times. Glance over your shoulder, watch the play, and be ready to organise your defence. Concentration must never drop

coaching_tips: Lower ability goalkeeper should stay close to goal at all times and not wander out
Teach all players never to blame the goalkeeper when the other team scores — this destroys confidence
Goalkeeper must communicate constantly — call for the ball, organise defenders, give instructions
Always kick or throw to the player on the wing, never up the middle
Have a backup goalkeeper trained and ready in case of injury
Keep focused on play in front when ball is not at your end — never switch off
Encourage goalkeeper after every save, even small ones — confidence is everything
Teach the goalkeeper to read the game — watch attacker's body shape to predict shot direction
Practice distribution daily — a goalkeeper who distributes well starts attacks
Remind goalkeeper: the ready position is the foundation of all good goalkeeping

team_principles.principle: Support the Player with the Ball
team_principles.description: Move into open space within passing distance or call for the ball
Pass out of your own half, do not dribble. Safer to pass quickly up field.
Use dribbling moves in opponent's penalty area. Encourage fakes and direct runs at goal.
Play wide to the wings and attack down side of field before moving toward goal.

team_principles.principle: Shoot Whenever You Have a Good Opportunity
team_principles.description: If you have a reasonable chance to score, shoot. Do not wait.
key_words: If You Have A Chance, Shoot

responsibilities: Throws ball out quickly to wings
Follows play up to halfway line
Stays on their side of field
Stays on opponent's side of field
Quickly throws or punts ball to wings
Moves up to halfway line when team has ball
Gets open for passes from defenders
Passes to forwards and runs to support
Takes ball down wing and crosses
